scenarios.title: Designing a Real-time Analytics Pipeline
scenarios.description: You need to design a system to ingest, process, and visualize clickstream data from a high-traffic product surface (e.g., Facebook News Feed). The system should handle peak loads of 10,000 events per second and provide real-time dashboards with a maximum delay of 5 seconds.
keyConsiderations: Scalability for traffic spikes (billions of events per day)
Fault tolerance and exactly-once semantics
Schema evolution handling via schema registry
Cost optimization through resource-aware scheduling
Data latency requirements (sub-5-second freshness)
scenarios.solutionApproach: A lambda architecture would be suitable, with: (1) Scribe for log ingestion from client applications, (2) A Flink-based streaming framework for real-time processing, (3) HDFS for raw data storage with Hive-compatible partitioning, (4) Spark batch jobs for historical reprocessing, (5) Scuba for real-time dashboards with sub-second query latency on pre-aggregated data.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Presto query: Real-time clickstream aggregation (runs every 5 min)
SELECT
  user_id,
  product_id,
  event_type,
  DATE_TRUNC('minute', event_timestamp) AS time_window,
  COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM clickstream_events
WHERE event_date = CURRENT_DATE
  AND event_timestamp >= NOW() - INTERVAL '5' MINUTE
GROUP BY
  user_id,
  product_id,
  event_type,
  DATE_TRUNC('minute', event_timestamp);

-- Flink streaming job (pseudocode)
val stream = scribeSource("clickstream_topic")
  .keyBy(event => event.userId)
  .window(TumblingEventTimeWindows.of(Time.minutes(1)))
  .aggregate(new EventCountAggregator())
  .addSink(hivePartitionedSink("/warehouse/clickstream_agg"))
scenarios.aiApproach: At Meta's scale, consider using Scuba for anomaly detection on real-time aggregated streams. Scuba can evaluate threshold-based alerts with sub-second latency on pre-ingested data, avoiding the need to process all raw events in a separate step. For deeper anomaly detection, a lightweight ML model running on the Flink streaming layer can flag unusual patterns and trigger immediate alerts without adding batch processing overhead.

scenarios.title: Building a Data Warehouse for Multiple Teams
scenarios.description: Your organization has diverse data needs across ads, product analytics, and integrity teams. Design a data warehouse architecture that provides a single source of truth while enabling team-specific analytics needs with appropriate governance.
systemComponents: Team Workspaces (team-specific schemas in the warehouse)
Governance Layer (data catalog + RBAC access controls)
keyConsiderations: Data quality controls at each layer
Access management via RBAC and data catalog policies
Metadata management through a centralized data catalog
Cost allocation across teams (compute and storage budgets)
Integration with existing tools (Presto, Spark, Scuba)
scenarios.solutionApproach: Implement a medallion architecture in the Hive data warehouse: (1) Bronze layer for raw data landed from Scribe into HDFS, (2) Silver layer for validated and cleansed tables (deduplication, schema enforcement), (3) Gold layer for business-level aggregate tables consumed by dashboards, (4) Data catalog for data discovery and governance, (5) RBAC access controls for team-specific zones ensuring compliance with data privacy policies.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Define the HDFS / Hive warehouse structure
-- /warehouse
--   /bronze
--     /scribe_logs/{YYYY}/{MM}/{DD}/raw_files
--     /api_ingestion/{YYYY}/{MM}/{DD}/raw_files
--   /silver
--     /dim_users/  (partitioned Hive table)
--     /dim_products/
--     /fact_events/
--   /gold
--     /ads/campaign_performance
--     /product/engagement_metrics
--     /integrity/abuse_signals

-- Spark job for bronze-to-silver transformation
df = spark.read.format("json").load("/warehouse/bronze/scribe_logs/2026/03/05/*")
df_clean = (
    df.dropDuplicates(["event_id"])
      .filter(col("user_id").isNotNull())
      .withColumn("event_date", to_date(col("event_timestamp")))
)
df_clean.write \
    .format("orc") \
    .mode("append") \
    .partitionBy("event_date") \
    .saveAsTable("silver.fact_events")
scenarios.aiApproach: Implement a data contract approach between teams, where each team defines its data requirements as a 'contract' that upstream producers must fulfill when modifying shared datasets. At Meta's scale, the data catalog enforces lineage tracking so breaking changes are caught before deployment. Use automated data profiling (row counts, null rates, distribution checks) at each layer transition to prevent bad data from propagating downstream.

scenarios.title: Optimizing a Slow Data Warehouse
scenarios.description: A core reporting pipeline on the Hive/Presto data warehouse is experiencing performance issues with month-end reporting queries taking 4+ hours. Redesign the system to bring reporting time under 30 minutes.
systemComponents: Storage Format Optimization (ORC/Parquet with columnar compression)
keyConsiderations: Partition key selection aligned with common query filters
Bucketing on high-cardinality join keys for co-located joins
Table statistics maintenance for the Presto query optimizer
Resource allocation (Presto cluster sizing, queue management)
Query patterns analysis to identify hot paths
scenarios.solutionApproach: Optimize by: (1) Converting fact tables to ORC columnar format with Zstandard compression for efficient scans, (2) Implementing bucketing on high-cardinality join keys (user_id) for co-located joins in Presto, (3) Partitioning large tables by date aligned with common WHERE clause filters, (4) Creating pre-aggregated summary tables (materialized as Hive tables refreshed hourly) for common aggregations, (5) Tuning Presto session properties (join distribution type, memory limits) for reporting workloads.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
-- Optimize table storage and partitioning
CREATE TABLE fact_sales (
  sales_key     BIGINT,
  customer_key  BIGINT,
  product_key   INT,
  quantity      INT,
  unit_price    DECIMAL(18,2),
  total_price   DECIMAL(18,2),
  event_date    DATE
)
PARTITIONED BY (event_date)
CLUSTERED BY (customer_key) INTO 256 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES ('orc.compress'='ZSTD');

-- Pre-aggregated summary table for common report
CREATE TABLE agg_monthly_sales_by_product AS
SELECT
  product_key,
  YEAR(event_date) AS sales_year,
  MONTH(event_date) AS sales_month,
  SUM(quantity) AS total_quantity,
  SUM(total_price) AS total_sales
FROM fact_sales
GROUP BY
  product_key,
  YEAR(event_date),
  MONTH(event_date);

-- Presto session tuning for large reporting queries
SET SESSION join_distribution_type = 'PARTITIONED';
SET SESSION query_max_memory_per_node = '8GB';

SELECT
  p.product_name,
  a.sales_year,
  a.sales_month,
  a.total_quantity,
  a.total_sales
FROM agg_monthly_sales_by_product a
JOIN dim_product p ON a.product_key = p.product_key
ORDER BY a.total_sales DESC;
scenarios.aiApproach: At Meta's scale, use Dataswarm (internal workflow scheduler) to schedule pre-aggregation jobs that run just before reporting windows, ensuring summary tables are fresh. For ad-hoc queries, leverage Presto's cost-based optimizer by running ANALYZE TABLE to keep statistics up to date. If month-end reporting is predictable, pre-compute the exact aggregations needed and serve them from a fast-path table — this converts a 4-hour scan into a sub-minute lookup.

scenarios.title: Designing a Multi-Region Data Replication Strategy
scenarios.description: For a global application serving billions of users, design a data architecture that ensures data consistency, disaster recovery with RPO < 5 minutes, and optimized read performance for users across different geographic regions.
systemComponents: Regional Replicas (read replicas in each region)
keyConsiderations: Regulatory compliance and data sovereignty
Data residency requirements per region
Latency requirements for read vs. write paths
Consistency model (strong vs. eventual)
Failover automation with minimal data loss
scenarios.solutionApproach: Implement: (1) A primary HDFS/Hive data center as the source of truth with synchronous writes, (2) Scribe-based log replication to regional data centers for near-real-time data sync, (3) Regional Presto clusters for low-latency reads from local replicas, (4) An automated failover system that promotes a secondary region to primary if heartbeat checks fail (RPO < 5 min via continuous log shipping), (5) Application-level conflict resolution for the rare cases of concurrent writes during failover.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
# Python: Region-aware data replication configuration
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List

@dataclass
class RegionConfig:
    region_name: str
    hdfs_endpoint: str
    presto_endpoint: str
    is_primary: bool
    failover_priority: int

regions = [
    RegionConfig('us-east', 'hdfs://us-east-nn:9000', 'presto://us-east:8080', True, 0),
    RegionConfig('us-west', 'hdfs://us-west-nn:9000', 'presto://us-west:8080', False, 1),
    RegionConfig('eu-west', 'hdfs://eu-west-nn:9000', 'presto://eu-west:8080', False, 2),
]

def get_read_endpoint(user_region: str) -> str:
    """Route reads to the nearest regional Presto cluster."""
    region = next((r for r in regions if r.region_name == user_region), None)
    return region.presto_endpoint if region else regions[0].presto_endpoint

def failover(failed_region: str) -> str:
    """Promote the next region in failover priority."""
    candidates = sorted(
        [r for r in regions if r.region_name != failed_region],
        key=lambda r: r.failover_priority
    )
    new_primary = candidates[0]
    new_primary.is_primary = True
    # Trigger Scribe log catchup to ensure < 5 min RPO
    trigger_log_catchup(new_primary.region_name)
    return new_primary.region_name

-- Presto: Check replication lag per region
SELECT
  region,
  MAX(event_timestamp) AS latest_event,
  NOW() - MAX(event_timestamp) AS replication_lag
FROM regional_replication_status
GROUP BY region
HAVING NOW() - MAX(event_timestamp) > INTERVAL '5' MINUTE;
scenarios.aiApproach: At Meta's scale, true multi-region consistency is achieved through a combination of synchronous replication for critical paths (e.g., financial transactions) and asynchronous replication for high-volume analytical data. Rather than relying solely on timestamp-based conflict resolution, implement application-level conflict resolution that understands the business semantics — for example, for user profile updates, the most recent edit wins, but for counter increments (like view counts), conflicts should be resolved by summing rather than overwriting.

scenarios.title: Migrating a Legacy Data Warehouse to a Modern Stack
scenarios.description: A team wants to migrate 15TB of data from a legacy on-premises data warehouse to the modern Hive/Presto/Spark stack with minimal downtime. The solution should address performance, security, and cost optimization while enabling future scalability.
systemComponents: Migration Assessment (schema audit, query pattern analysis)
Target Architecture (Hive tables on HDFS, Presto for queries)
keyConsiderations: Data transfer approach (bulk initial load + incremental sync)
Schema compatibility between legacy types and Hive types
Historical data handling (backfill vs. archive)
Business continuity during the migration window
Cost management (compute budget for migration jobs)
scenarios.solutionApproach: Implement a phased migration: (1) Audit the legacy schema and query patterns to map to Hive table designs, (2) Use Dataswarm to orchestrate Spark jobs that extract data from the legacy system, (3) Land data into Hive tables on HDFS with ORC format and appropriate partitioning, (4) Implement incremental sync using a watermark column (last_modified_date), (5) Run dual-write during transition so both old and new systems serve queries, (6) Validate with automated reconciliation queries comparing row counts and key metrics.
scenarios.pseudoCode:
# Spark job: Incremental migration from legacy DB to Hive
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("LegacyMigration").getOrCreate()

# Read the last successful watermark
last_load_date = spark.sql("""
    SELECT MAX(watermark_value) AS hw
    FROM migration_metadata.load_watermarks
    WHERE table_name = 'fact_orders'
""").collect()[0]['hw']

# Extract incremental data from legacy system via JDBC
incremental_df = spark.read.format("jdbc").options(
    url="jdbc:postgresql://legacy-db:5432/warehouse",
    dbtable=f"(SELECT * FROM fact_orders WHERE last_modified > '{last_load_date}') AS inc",
    driver="org.postgresql.Driver"
).load()

# Transform: normalize types, add metadata
transformed_df = (
    incremental_df
    .withColumn("order_date", col("order_date").cast("date"))
    .withColumn("dbt_loaded_at", current_timestamp())
)

# Load into Hive (UPSERT via INSERT OVERWRITE on the target partition)
transformed_df.write \
    .format("orc") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("order_date") \
    .saveAsTable("warehouse.staging_fact_orders")

# Update watermark
spark.sql(f"""
    INSERT INTO migration_metadata.load_watermarks
    VALUES ('fact_orders', '{new_watermark}', CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
""")

-- Reconciliation query (Presto, run daily during parallel period)
SELECT
  'row_count' AS metric,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM legacy_warehouse.fact_orders) AS legacy_value,
  (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM warehouse.fact_orders) AS new_value,
  ABS(
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM legacy_warehouse.fact_orders)
    - (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM warehouse.fact_orders)
  ) AS diff
UNION ALL
SELECT
  'total_revenue' AS metric,
  (SELECT CAST(SUM(order_amount) AS DOUBLE) FROM legacy_warehouse.fact_orders),
  (SELECT CAST(SUM(order_amount) AS DOUBLE) FROM warehouse.fact_orders),
  ABS(
    (SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM legacy_warehouse.fact_orders)
    - (SELECT SUM(order_amount) FROM warehouse.fact_orders)
  );
scenarios.aiApproach: Consider a 'lift and improve' approach instead of 'lift and shift', using the migration as an opportunity to redesign problematic schemas and implement modern data patterns like slowly changing dimensions (SCD Type 2) in the Hive warehouse. At Meta's scale, the biggest risk is the cutover window — always run dual-write for at least 2 weeks with automated reconciliation before cutting over. Use Dataswarm to orchestrate both the migration jobs and the reconciliation checks as a single workflow with alerting on drift.
